sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presented with a sore on his right foot that has not been healing for approximately two to three weeks.
He initially saw his primary care physician, who referred him to me.
He reported that he had previously seen a podiatrist but was unable to find a replacement due to their relocation.
The wound is located on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, and is approximately 1 by 2 inches in size.
He denied any trauma to the foot, and the wound does not hurt despite his neuropathy.
Past Medical History: The patient has a history of diabetes, with a last HbA1c of over 8%.
He also mentioned that he has neuropathy.
Review of Systems: The patient denied any symptoms such as fever, chills, drainage from the wound, or other symptoms.
Current Medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital Signs: Temperature 98.1°F, heart rate 72, respirations 16, blood pressure 110/65.
Physical Exam: The patient has a 1 by 2 inch circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, with yellow slough present and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, no necrosis, and no odor.
The wound does not hurt when touched.
The vascular exam revealed palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, but no palpable DP or PT pulses, with Doppler signs present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray showed no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient presents with a diabetic foot ulcer that requires further evaluation and treatment to promote healing.
Problem List: Diabetic foot ulcer; diabetes mellitus; neuropathy.
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily and covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I will order an ankle-brachial index (ABI) to determine the blood supply to the foot.
I will have the patient wear a surgical shoe to take pressure off the area.
I do not think antibiotics are needed at this time.
I will see the patient again in two weeks and may refer him to a vascular specialist if the ABI indicates poor blood supply for wound healing.
I will also refer the patient to a diabetic specialist to discuss ways to modify his diet to improve his HbA1c levels.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for a follow-up appointment.
